2188A - Divisible Permutation

  • Idea: __baozii__, Tobo

  • Solution: __baozii__

  • Editorial: __baozii__

Solution

For |pn−1−pn||pn−1−pn| to be divisible by n−1n−1, either pn−1=1pn−1=1 and pn=npn=n, or pn−1=npn−1=n and pn=1pn=1. Either way, we can construct pp backwards. For ii from n−2n−2 to 11, there will only by one possible choice for pipi, where pipi is either pi+1−ipi+1−i or pi+1+ipi+1+i, depending on the parity of ii.

Code (Python)
for _ in range(int(input())):
    n = int(input())
    p = [1, n]
    used = {1, n}
    for i in range(n - 2, 0, -1):
        prev = p[-1]
        if 1 <= prev - i <= n and prev - i not in used:
            p.append(prev - i)
            used.add(prev - i)
        else:
            p.append(prev + i)
            used.add(prev + i)
    p.reverse()
    print(*p)

2188B - Seats

  • Idea: -firefly-

  • Solution: -firefly-

  • Editorial: -firefly-

Solution

We can divide the string into segments that contain only zeros. For one segment that is bounded by two ones, the answer is ⌊len3⌋⌊len3⌋.

Proof

Each new student added to the segment can cover a maximum of 33 empty seats (the seat they occupy plus one on each side).

For every 33 zeros, we must place exactly 11 student to satisfy the condition while keeping the total count minimal.

After placing these ⌊len3⌋⌊len3⌋ students, we can let the remaining zeros placed at the boundaries so that they're covered by the neighbor $1$s.

If a segment of zeros is adjacent to the start or the end of the row, we need to introduce extra zeros. This is because in the proof, the boundary that the remaining zeros can be placed are reduced. If the whole string consist of zeors, we need to introduce two zeros. Otherwise, we need to introduce one zero.

By summing up the contributions of the segments, we can solve the answer.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using i64 = long long;

void solve() {
    int n;
    std::cin >> n;
    std::string s;
    std::cin >> s;
    s = '1' + s + '1';
    int ans = 0;
    for (int i = 1, l = 0; i <= n; ++i) {
        if (s[i] == '0') {
            if (s[i-1] == '1') {
                l = i;
            }
            if (s[i+1] == '1') {
                int c = (l == 1) + (i == n);
                ans += (i - l + 1 + c) / 3;
            }
        } else {
            ++ans;
        }
    }
    std::cout << ans << '\n';
}

int main() {
    std::cin.tie(nullptr)->sync_with_stdio(false);
    int T;
    std::cin >> T;
    while (T--) solve();
    return 0;
}

2187A - Restricted Sorting

  • Idea: __baozii__

  • Solution: __baozii__

  • Editorial: __baozii__

Hint 1

For any array of elements, if you can somehow swap any pair of elements (maybe indirectly), you can always sort the array.

Hint 2

For some i,ji,j such that |ai−aj|<k|ai−aj|<k, how to swap them with help from other elements?

Solution

2187A - Restricted Sorting

Firstly, if aa is already sorted, then any integer kk is piggy, we can simply output −1−1.

Let bb be the array obtained by sorting aa. For some 1≤i≤n1≤i≤n, if ai=biai=bi, we do not need to touch it. For ii with ai≠biai≠bi, we need to swap them to their correct places. Let mx=max(a)mx=max(a) and mn=min(a)mn=min(a). Consider for an integer kk, how do we check if it is piggy:

  • If mx−mn<kmx−mn<k, we can swap none of the elements, so kk is not piggy.
  • If there exists some ii such that ai≠biai≠bi and mx−ai<kmx−ai<k and ai−mn<kai−mn<k, we can never swap this element, so kk is not piggy.
  • Otherwise, we claim that kk is piggy. This is because we can indirectly swap any pair of aiai and ajaj, with help from mnmn and mxmx. Without loss of generality, consider two cases:
    • If ai−mn≥kai−mn≥k and aj−mn≥kaj−mn≥k, we can perform the following swaps (elements are shown by their relation positions):
      • aiai, ajaj, mnmn
      • aiai, mnmn, ajaj
      • mnmn, aiai, ajaj
      • ajaj, aiai, mnmn
    • If ai−mn≥kai−mn≥k and mx−aj≥kmx−aj≥k:
      • aiai, ajaj, mnmn, mxmx
      • mnmn, ajaj, aiai, mxmx
      • mxmx, ajaj, aiai, mnmn
      • ajaj, mxmx, aiai, mnmn
      • ajaj, mnmn, aiai, mxmx
      • ajaj, aiai, mnmn, mxmx

    As shown in Hint 1, if we can swap any two elements, we can always sort the array.

With the above analysis, we can binary search for the largest piggy kk, and check the answer in O(n)O(n), with overall complexity O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n). In fact, we do not even need to perform binary search. The answer can be shown to be:

minai≠bi(max(ai−mn,mx−ai))minai≠bi(max(ai−mn,mx−ai))

Code (Python)
for _ in range(int(input())):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = sorted(a)
    if a == b:
        print(-1)
        continue
    print(min(max(a[i] - b[0], b[-1] - a[i]) for i in range(n) if a[i] != b[i]))

2187B - Shortest Statement Ever

  • Idea: __baozii__

  • Solution: __baozii__

  • Editorial: __baozii__, rucaco

Hint 1

Greedy.

Solution

2187B - Shortest Statement Ever

This is the intended solution.

Find the highest bit bb such that both pp and qq have a 11. We need to modify pp and qq such that one of pp or qq has a 00 on this bit. Without loss of generality, there are two ways to do this:

  • Increment pp until this bit is 00, and let y=qy=q. In this case, if x&y=0x&y=0, update the answer.
  • Let x=..high bits same as p100..000b−1 zeroesx=..⏟high bits same as p100..000⏟b−1 zeroes, and y=..high bits same as q011..111b−1 onesy=..⏟high bits same as q011..111⏟b−1 ones. It can be shown that x&y=0x&y=0.

It can be proven that this greedy leads to an optimal solution. Overall complexity O(1)O(1) per test case.

There is another interesting fact: there always exists a solution where p=xp=x or q=yq=y. You may also build a greedy solution on top of this observation.

There is also a dynamic programming solution, which is significantly more tedious.

Proof of the interesting fact

Let x,y∈Z≥0x,y∈Z≥0.
We consider the optimization problem

minp,q∈Z≥0|x−p|+|y−q|subject top&q=0,minp,q∈Z≥0|x−p|+|y−q|subject top&q=0,

where && denotes the bitwise AND operation.

Equivalent Formulation

Define

OPT(x,y):=minp,q≥0p&q=0(|x−p|+|y−q|).OPT(x,y):=minp,q≥0p&q=0(|x−p|+|y−q|).

Define also the restricted optima

OPTx(x,y):=minq≥0x&q=0|y−q|,OPTy(x,y):=minp≥0p&y=0|x−p|.OPTx(x,y):=minq≥0x&q=0|y−q|,OPTy(x,y):=minp≥0p&y=0|x−p|.

Proposition

OPT(x,y)=min{OPTx(x,y),OPTy(x,y)}OPT(x,y)=min{OPTx(x,y),OPTy(x,y)}

Equivalently:

There exists an optimal solution (p,q)(p,q) such that p=xp=x or q=yq=y.

Proof of Equivalence

  • If an optimal solution satisfies p=xp=x, then its cost equals |y−q||y−q| for some qq with x&q=0x&q=0, hence OPT=OPTxOPT=OPTx.
  • Similarly, if an optimal solution satisfies q=yq=y, then OPT=OPTyOPT=OPTy.
  • Conversely, if the equality holds, the minimizer in the attaining branch gives an optimal solution with p=xp=x or q=yq=y.

Inductive Proof on Bit-Length

Fix an integer n≥1n≥1.
Assume

0≤x,y<2n,0≤p,q<2n.0≤x,y<2n,0≤p,q<2n.

Let

W:=2n−1.W:=2n−1.

Write the binary decompositions

x=x1W+x0,y=y1W+y0,x=x1W+x0,y=y1W+y0,
p=p1W+p0,q=q1W+q0,p=p1W+p0,q=q1W+q0,

where

x1,y1,p1,q1∈{0,1},x0,y0,p0,q0∈[0,W).x1,y1,p1,q1∈{0,1},x0,y0,p0,q0∈[0,W).

The constraint p&q=0p&q=0 implies:

  1. p1p1 and q1q1 cannot both be 11,
  2. p0&q0=0p0&q0=0.

Case 1: No Conflict (x1,y1)≠(1,1)(x1,y1)≠(1,1)

In this case, the highest bit does not force a trade-off.

Lemma 1

If x1=0x1=0, then there exists an optimal solution with p1=0p1=0. Similarly, if y1=0y1=0, there exists an optimal solution with q1=0q1=0.

Proof

Assume x1=0x1=0 but p1=1p1=1. Replace pp by p′=p−Wp′=p−W.

  • Feasibility: clearing a bit of pp cannot create a new 1&11&1 conflict.
  • Optimality: since x<Wx<W,
|x−p′|=|x−(p−W)|≤|x−p|.|x−p′|=|x−(p−W)|≤|x−p|.

Thus p1p1 can be set to 00 without increasing cost.
The argument for qq is symmetric.

Hence we may assume

p1=x1,q1=y1.p1=x1,q1=y1.

The objective becomes

|x−p|+|y−q|=|x0−p0|+|y0−q0|.|x−p|+|y−q|=|x0−p0|+|y0−q0|.

This reduces the problem to the (n−1)(n−1)-bit instance (x0,y0)(x0,y0). By the inductive hypothesis, there exists an optimal solution with p0=x0p0=x0 or q0=y0q0=y0, hence p=xp=x or q=yq=y.

Case 2: Conflict (x1,y1)=(1,1)(x1,y1)=(1,1)

Now the highest bit conflicts, so

(p1,q1)∈{(1,0),(0,1),(0,0)}.(p1,q1)∈{(1,0),(0,1),(0,0)}.

Lemma 2

We could Exclusion of (0,0)(0,0).

Proof

Any feasible solution with

p,q∈[0,W)p,q∈[0,W)

satisfies

|x−p|+|y−q|≥W+x0+y0+1.|x−p|+|y−q|≥W+x0+y0+1.

Moreover, there exists a feasible solution with (p1,q1)=(1,0)(p1,q1)=(1,0) and cost

x0+y0+1.x0+y0+1.

Since x=W+x0>px=W+x0>p and y=W+y0>qy=W+y0>q,

|x−p|+|y−q|=(W+x0−p)+(W+y0−q)=2W+x0+y0−(p+q).|x−p|+|y−q|=(W+x0−p)+(W+y0−q)=2W+x0+y0−(p+q).

If p &amp; q = 0p &amp; q = 0, then binary addition has no carry, hence

p+q=p⊕q=p∣q≤W−1.p+q=p⊕q=p∣q≤W−1.

Therefore

|x−p|+|y−q|≥2W+x0+y0−(W−1)=W+x0+y0+1.|x−p|+|y−q|≥2W+x0+y0−(W−1)=W+x0+y0+1.

Now define

p′:=x=W+x0,q′:=W−1−x0.p′:=x=W+x0,q′:=W−1−x0.

Since q′q′ is the bitwise complement of x0x0 on the lower n−1n−1 bits,

p′&q′=x0&(W−1−x0)=0.p′&q′=x0&(W−1−x0)=0.

The cost is

|x−p′|+|y−q′|=0+((W+y0)−(W−1−x0))=x0+y0+1.|x−p′|+|y−q′|=0+((W+y0)−(W−1−x0))=x0+y0+1.

Since W≥1W≥1,

W+x0+y0+1>x0+y0+1.W+x0+y0+1>x0+y0+1.

Thus the (0,0)(0,0) case cannot be optimal.

Lemma 3

Fix (p1,q1)=(1,0)(p1,q1)=(1,0). Then there exists an optimal solution with p=xp=x.

Proof

Write p=W+p0p=W+p0, q=q0q=q0. The cost is

|x0−p0|+(W+y0−q0).|x0−p0|+(W+y0−q0).

For fixed p0p0, this is minimized by maximizing q0q0 subject to p0&q0=0p0&q0=0, i.e.

q0=W−1−p0.q0=W−1−p0.

The resulting cost is

y0+1+(p0+|x0−p0|).y0+1+(p0+|x0−p0|).

Define g(t)=t+|x0−t|g(t)=t+|x0−t|. Then

g(t)={x0,2t−x0,t≤x0,t≥x0.g(t)={x0,t≤x0,2t−x0,t≥x0.

Hence the minimum is attained at t=x0t=x0, yielding p=xp=x.

By symmetry, in the (0,1)(0,1) case there exists an optimal solution with q=yq=y.

Conclusion

In all cases, there exists an optimal solution (p,q)(p,q) satisfying

p=xorq=y.p=xorq=y.

Equivalently,

OPT(x,y)=min{OPTx(x,y),OPTy(x,y)}.OPT(x,y)=min{OPTx(x,y),OPTy(x,y)}.

Proofs of the intended solution are similar with this.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using i64 = long long;

void solve() {
    i64 x, y;
    std::cin >> x >> y;
    i64 min = 1e18, p, q;
    auto upd = [&](i64 _p, i64 _q) -> void {
        if ((_p & _q) == 0 && abs(_p - x) + abs(_q - y) < min) {
            min = abs(_p - x) + abs(_q - y);
            p = _p;
            q = _q;
        }  
    };
    upd(x, y);
    for (int i = 29; i >= 0; i--) {
        if ((x & y) >> i & 1) {
            upd((x >> i << i) + (1LL << i), y);
            upd(x, (y >> i << i) + (1LL << i));
            upd(x >> i << i, (y >> i << i) - 1);
            upd((x >> i << i) - 1, y >> i << i);
        }
    }
    std::cout << p << " " << q << "\n";
}

int main() {
    std::ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    std::cin.tie(nullptr);
    std::cout.tie(nullptr);

    int t;
    std::cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        solve();
    }
}

2187C - Jerry and Tom

  • Idea: Bronya_H, __baozii__

  • Solution: __baozii__

  • Editorial: __baozii__

Hint 1

What is the optimal strategy for both players?

Hint 2

If you have obtained a formula for f(x,y)f(x,y), how do you calculate the required sum efficiently, perhaps using some tree techniques?

Solution

2187C - Jerry and Tom

Observation: Both players will always take the outgoing edge that goes the furthest.

Suppose there are two edges (u→v)(u→v) and (u→w)(u→w) with v<wv<w. If a player takes the edge (u→v)(u→v), due to the special property of GG (no crossing edges), the player will never reach any vertices beyond ww faster than if he were to take the edge (u→w)(u→w).

Let the furthest vertex reachable from uu be a(u)a(u). If we connect all edges (u→a(u))(u→a(u)), we get a tree rooted at nn. Let the depth of uu be d(u)d(u). There are two cases:

  • If d(x)<d(y)d(x)<d(y), Tom can never catch Jerry before Jerry reaches nn, thus f(x,y)=0f(x,y)=0.
  • Otherwise, Tom can move to the lowest common ancestor (lca) of xx and yy, denoted by lca(x,y)lca⁡(x,y). To reach nn, Jerry eventually has to move to lca(x,y)lca⁡(x,y), where Tom will catch him. Thus, f(x,y)=d(y)−d(lca(x,y))f(x,y)=d(y)−d(lca⁡(x,y)).

Simplifying things a little:

∑1≤x,y≤n,x≠yf(x,y)=∑1≤x,y≤n,x≠y,d(x)≥d(y)(d(y)−d(lca(x,y)))=∑1≤x<y≤n(min(d(x),d(y))−d(lca(x,y)))+∑1≤x<y≤n,d(x)=d(y)(d(y)−d(lca(x,y)))=∑1≤x<y≤nmin(d(x),d(y))−∑1≤x<y≤nd(lca(x,y))−∑1≤x<y≤n,d(x)=d(y)(d(y)−d(lca(x,y)))∑1≤x,y≤n,x≠yf(x,y)=∑1≤x,y≤n,x≠y,d(x)≥d(y)(d(y)−d(lca⁡(x,y)))=∑1≤x<y≤n(min(d(x),d(y))−d(lca⁡(x,y)))+∑1≤x<y≤n,d(x)=d(y)(d(y)−d(lca⁡(x,y)))=∑1≤x<y≤nmin(d(x),d(y))−∑1≤x<y≤nd(lca⁡(x,y))−∑1≤x<y≤n,d(x)=d(y)(d(y)−d(lca⁡(x,y)))

The first sum can be calculated by sorting the depth array. Let the sorted depth array be bb, then the first sum is ∑ni=1(n−i−1)⋅bi∑i=1n(n−i−1)⋅bi.

The second sum can be calculated by precomputing subtree sizes. Let the size of the subtree of uu be s(u)s(u). The number of pairs of vertices with lca=ulca=u is ∑v,w∈children of us(v)⋅s(w)=s(u)⋅(s(u)−1)2−∑v∈children of us(v)⋅(s(v)−1)2∑v,w∈children of us(v)⋅s(w)=s(u)⋅(s(u)−1)2−∑v∈children of us(v)⋅(s(v)−1)2.

The third sum can be calculated similarly, by separating the two components. There are many ways to optimize this process. The standard solution uses dsu on tree, with overall complexity O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n).

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
 
using namespace std;
 
using i64 = long long;
 
i64 solve_tree(int n, int r, vector<vector<int>> g) {
    vector<int> dep(n), sz(n, 1), pa(n);
    i64 ans = 0;
    auto dfs = [&](auto &&self, int u, int p) -> map<int, int> {
        pa[u] = p;
        map<int, int> mp;
        mp[dep[u]] = 1;
        for (auto v : g[u]) if (v != p) {
            dep[v] = dep[u] + 1;
            auto nmp = self(self, v, u);
            if (mp.size() < nmp.size()) swap(mp, nmp);
            for (auto &[d, f] : nmp) {
                ans += 1LL * f * mp[d] * (d - dep[u]);
                mp[d] += f;
            }
            sz[u] += sz[v];
        }
        return mp;
    };
    dfs(dfs, r, -1);
    for (int u = 0; u < n; u++) {
        i64 s = 1LL * sz[u] * (sz[u] - 1) / 2;
        for (auto v : g[u]) if (v != pa[u]) s -= 1LL * sz[v] * (sz[v] - 1) / 2;
        ans -= 1LL * dep[u] * s;
    }
    sort(dep.begin(), dep.end());
    for (int u = 0; u < n; u++) ans += 1LL * dep[u] * (n - u - 1);
    return ans;
}
 
void solve() {
    int n, m;
    cin >> n >> m;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) a[i] = i + 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        int u, v;
        cin >> u >> v;
        u--;
        v--;
        a[u] = max(a[u], v);
    }
    vector<vector<int>> g(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) g[a[i]].push_back(i);
    cout << solve_tree(n, n - 1, g) << "\n";
}
 
int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
 
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}
Bonus

Solve the problem in O(n)O(n).

Rate the problem!
Quality
  • 146 Excellent problem
  • 11 Good problem
  • 5 Average problem
  • 4 Bad problem
  • 5 Horrible problem
Difficulty
  • 4 Trivial problem
  • 2 Easy problem
  • 21 Average problem
  • 101 Hard problem
  • 9 Impossible problem

2187D - Cool Problem

  • Idea: Error_Yuan

  • Solution: Error_Yuan

  • Editorial: Error_Yuan

Solution

2187D - Cool Problem

Note that we can rewrite the formula as follows:

  • ci−x+y2=ci−1−y−x2ci−x+y2=ci−1−y−x2, if si=0si=0;
  • ci−x+y2=y−x2−ci−1ci−x+y2=y−x2−ci−1, if si=1si=1.

From this, we can see that

(ci−x+y2)2=(ci−1−y−x2)2(ci−x+y2)2=(ci−1−y−x2)2

always holds. Thus,

(c2i−c2i−1)−y⋅(ci−ci−1)+xy=x⋅(ci+ci−1)(ci2−ci−12)−y⋅(ci−ci−1)+xy=x⋅(ci+ci−1)

So,

∑(c2i−c2i−1)−∑y⋅(ci−ci−1)+nxy=x⋅∑(ci+ci−1)∑(ci2−ci−12)−∑y⋅(ci−ci−1)+nxy=x⋅∑(ci+ci−1)

This implies

f(r)=c2n+(x−y)⋅cn+nxy2xf(r)=cn2+(x−y)⋅cn+nxy2x

Thus, we can use dynamic programming to calculate all reachable cncn. Note that the coefficient of yy in cncn is either 11 or 00, we may define dpi,j,kdpi,j,k as whether the coefficient of xx can be jj, considering the first ii characters of ss, and the current coefficient of yy is kk, which can be optimized to O(n2w)O(n2w) using bitset.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

using B = bitset<100001>;

using i64 = long long;
using i128 = __int128_t;

constexpr i64 MOD = 998244353;

vector<i64> calc(int n, string &s, i64 x, i64 y) {
    B bf0, bf1, br0, br1;
    bf0.set(0);
    br0.set(0);
    for (auto c : s) {
        B nbf0, nbf1, nbr0, nbr1;
        if (c != '1') {
            nbf0 |= bf0 << 1;
            nbf1 |= bf1 << 1;
            nbr0 |= br0 >> 1;
            nbr1 |= br1 >> 1;
        }
        if (c != '0') {
            nbf0 |= br1;
            nbf1 |= br0;
            nbr0 |= bf1;
            nbr1 |= bf0;
        }
        nbf0[0] = nbf0[0] | nbr0[0];
        nbr0[0] = nbr0[0] | nbf0[0];
        nbf1[0] = nbf1[0] | nbr1[0];
        nbr1[0] = nbr1[0] | nbf1[0];
        bf0 = nbf0;
        bf1 = nbf1;
        br0 = nbr0;
        br1 = nbr1;
    }
    vector<i64> res;
    auto tosum = [&](i64 c) -> i64 {
        return (i128(c) * c + i128(x - y) * c + i128(n) * x * y) / (2 * x);
    };
    for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++) {
        if (bf0[i]) res.push_back(tosum(i * x));
        if (bf1[i]) res.push_back(tosum(i * x + y));
    }
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        if (br0[i]) res.push_back(tosum(-i * x));
        if (br1[i]) res.push_back(tosum(-i * x + y));
    }
    sort(res.begin(), res.end());
    res.erase(unique(res.begin(), res.end()), res.end());
    return res;
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    i64 x, y;
    cin >> x >> y;
    string s;
    cin >> s;
    auto rs = calc(n, s, x, y);
    i64 ans = 0;
    for (auto v : rs) ans = (ans + (v % MOD + MOD)) % MOD;
    cout << ans << "\n";
}

int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);

    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();

}
Rate the problem!
Quality
  • 23 Excellent problem
  • 16 Good problem
  • 6 Average problem
  • 9 Bad problem
  • 34 Horrible problem
Difficulty

  • 0 Easy problem
  • 5 Average problem
  • 43 Hard problem
  • 18 Impossible problem

2187E - Doors and Keys

  • Idea: StarSilk

  • Solution: StarSilk

  • Editorial: __baozii__

Hint 1

Can you transform some constraints of the problem into something easier to handle?

Hint 2

Try to come up with a dynamic programming solution that runs in polynomial time.

Hint 3

How do you optimize your solution? Pay attention to any unusual constraints.

Solution

2187E - Doors and Keys

Important Transformation: The constraint of carrying at most one key is annoying. It can actually be transformed into something else:

  • You may carry any number of keys with you. However, the time required to move to an adjacent room is max(1,2k−1)max(1,2k−1), where kk is the number of keys you can carrying.

This is because you can repeatedly move between rooms ii and i+1i+1, which requires kk trips to take all the keys with you.

Thus, we can design a dynamic programming solution. Let dpi,jdpi,j be the minimum time required to reach room ii, carrying jj keys. The base case is dp0,0=0dp0,0=0.

Consider the transitions:

  • If there is a key in room ii, you can choose to carry it with you or not. Thus, dpi,j+1=min(dpi,j+1,dpi,j)dpi,j+1=min(dpi,j+1,dpi,j).
  • If dpi,j≥aidpi,j≥ai, door ii is already open. Thus, dpi+1,j=min(dpi+1,j,dpi,j+max(1,2j−1))dpi+1,j=min(dpi+1,j,dpi,j+max(1,2j−1)).
  • Otherwise:
    • We can use one key to open door ii. Thus, dpi+1,j−1=min(dpi+1,j−1,dpi,j+max(1,2j−1))dpi+1,j−1=min(dpi+1,j−1,dpi,j+max(1,2j−1)).
    • We can also wait for door ii to open automatically at t=ait=ai. Thus, dpi+1,j=min(dpi+1,j,ai+max(1,2j−1))dpi+1,j=min(dpi+1,j,ai+max(1,2j−1)).

The above solution runs in O(n2)O(n2). Suppose you are carrying kk keys; in an optimal solution, all of these keys need to be used, otherwise you can simply carry fewer keys to achieve a not worse solution. The time required to move while carrying those keys is at least k+(k−1)+(k−2)+…+1=O(k2)k+(k−1)+(k−2)+…+1=O(k2). Thus, if we let V=max(a)V=max(a), it is never optimal to carry more than O(V−−√)O(V) keys, as the doors would have opened automatically by then. The second dimension will be only up to O(V−−√)O(V). The overall complexity should be O(nmin(n,V−−√))O(nmin(n,V)).

Code (Python)
for _ in range(int(input())):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    s = input()
    f = [10 ** 9] * 320
    f[0] = 0
    for i in range(n):
        if s[i] == "1":
            for j in range(318, -1, -1):
                f[j + 1] = min(f[j + 1], f[j])
        nf = [10 ** 9] * 320
        for j in range(320):
            nf[j] = min(nf[j], max(f[j], a[i]) + max(1, 2 * j - 1))
            if j:
                nf[j - 1] = min(nf[j - 1], f[j] + 2 * j - 1)
        print(nf[0]) 
        f = nf
Bonus

Solve the problem in O(npolylogn)O(npolylog⁡n).

2187F1 - Al Fine (Maximizing Version)

  • Idea: -firefly-

  • Solution: -firefly-

  • Editorial: -firefly-

Hint 1

Processing two permutations is difficult. How can we reduce them to one?

Answer

To simplify the problem, we can relabel the vertices such that one of the DFS orders becomes the identity permutation [0,1,2,…,n][0,1,2,…,n].

Specifically, let mp[v]mp[v] be the index of vertex vv in Arietta's DFS order bb. We then transform Tortinita's DFS order aa into a new sequence cc, where ci=mp[ai]ci=mp[ai]. After this transformation:

  • Arietta's sequence is effectively normalized to 0,1,2,…,n0,1,2,…,n.
  • Tortinita's sequence cc now represents the relative positions of her nodes in Arietta's tree.

This reduces the problem from handling two arbitrary permutations to analyzing a single permutation cc relative to the natural order.

Hint 2

In a DFS order, the nodes of any subtree form a contiguous segment, with the root of the subtree appearing at the very beginning of that segment. What property does this imply for our problem?

Answer

After relabeling, any subtree in Arietta's tree not only forms a contiguous segment in terms of positions but also in terms of values (i.e., the set of values will be [L,L+1,…,R][L,L+1,…,R]).

This implies that for a common subtree to exist, if a segment [L,L+1,…,R][L,L+1,…,R] represents a subtree in Arietta's tree, there must exist a corresponding segment in Tortinita's DFS order cc such that:

  • The first element of the segment is ll.
  • The remaining r−lr−l elements are a permutation of the set L+1,L+2,…,RL+1,L+2,…,R.
Hint 3

The property that a segment must be a "permutation of a specific set" is not straightforward to check directly. Is there a way to simplify this condition into something more computationally efficient?

Answer

We can simplify this condition by leveraging the relationship between the maximum, minimum, and the length of the segment.

For a subsegment of cc starting at index ll and ending at index rr (i.e., c[l…r]c[l…r]), it represents a common subtree if and only if:

  • c[l]=min(c[l…r])c[l]=min(c[l…r]): The first element must be the minimum value in the segment (acting as the root).
  • max(c[l…r])−min(c[l…r])=r−lmax(c[l…r])−min(c[l…r])=r−l: The range of values is exactly equal to the number of edges, meaning the values are consecutive.
Hint 4

Because each valid segment can be mapped to a subtree, the valid segments form a laminar family (i.e., they do not cross). Any two valid segments are either completely disjoint or one is strictly contained inside the other.

Hint 5

The problem asks for the largest depth. To achieve this, we can greedily find the largest valid segment for every fixed start position.

Solution

Read the hints above.

Recall that we need to solve for any 1≤l≤n1≤l≤n, the largest rr such that:

  • c[l]=min(c[l…r])c[l]=min(c[l…r])
  • max(c[l…r])−min(c[l…r])=r−lmax(c[l…r])−min(c[l…r])=r−l

There are multiple ways to solve this. We will introduce a divide-and-conquer approach.

To solve this problem using a divide-and-conquer approach, we define a recursive function that processes the interval [l,r][l,r]. The core logic handles the sub-problems where the valid range [i,j][i,j] crosses the midpoint midmid (with l≤i≤mid<j≤rl≤i≤mid<j≤r).

We precompute the prefix minimums and maximums for the right half (mid+1…rmid+1…r) and the suffix minimums and maximums for the left half (l…midl…mid). Let's denote the suffix values for index ii as min[i]min[i] and max[i]max[i], and similarly for the prefix values at index jj.

The problem requires c[i]=min(c[i…j])c[i]=min(c[i…j]). Since we are iterating ii from midmid down to ll, this condition implies that c[i]c[i] must be the minimum of the left part c[i…mid]c[i…mid]. If min[i]≠min[i+1]min[i]≠min[i+1], it indicates that c[i]c[i] is strictly smaller than the minimum of the range to its right, making it a candidate for the global minimum.

For such valid ii, we consider two cases based on the location of the maximum value in the range [i,j][i,j]:

Case 1: The Maximum is on the Left

If the maximum value of the range is determined by the left side (i.e., max(c[i…j])=max[i]max(c[i…j])=max[i]), the equation max−min=j−imax−min=j−i can be rewritten to determine the specific target index jj:

j=i+max[i]−min[i]j=i+max[i]−min[i]

We simply calculate this candidate jj and verify three conditions:

  • jj is within the valid right range (mid<j≤rmid<j≤r).
  • The minimum on the right does not contradict our global minimum assumption (min[j]>min[i]min[j]>min[i]).
  • The maximum on the right is not larger than our current maximum (max[j]<max[i]max[j]<max[i]).

If these hold, we update the answer for ii.

Case 2: The Maximum is on the Right

If the maximum value is on the right side (i.e., max(c[i…j])=max[j]max(c[i…j])=max[j]), the equation becomes:

max[j]−min[i]=j−imax[j]−min[i]=j−i

Rearranging the terms to separate ii and jj, we get:

max[j]−j=min[i]−imax[j]−j=min[i]−i

To solve this efficiently, we group the indices jj from the right half based on the value of max[j]−jmax[j]−j. For each ii, we simply access the corresponding group to find the largest jj that satisfies the remaining validity conditions (min[j]>min[i]min[j]>min[i] and max[j]>max[i]max[j]>max[i]). Because max[j]max[j] is monotonic, we can introduce two-pointer strategy to avoid the loglog factor.

By conducting this algorithm, we can solve the largest valid segments for each start position. Then, we can use a stack to simulate the dfs process of the common tree, and solve the maximum depth easily.

The time complexity is O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) by careful implementation.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using i64 = long long;

void solve() {
    int n;
    std::cin >> n;
    std::vector<int> a(n+1), b(n+1);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) std::cin >> a[i];
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) std::cin >> b[i];
    std::vector<int> mp(n+1), c(n+1), R(n+1);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) mp[b[i]] = i;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) c[i] = mp[a[i]];
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) mp[c[i]] = i;
    std::vector<int> min(n + 1), max(n + 1);
    std::vector<std::vector<std::pair<int, int>>> rmax(2 * n + 1);
    std::vector<int> p(2 * n + 1);

    auto work = [&](auto &&self, int l, int r) -> void {
        if (l >= r) return;
        int mid = l + (r - l) / 2;
        self(self, l, mid);
        self(self, mid + 1, r);
        min[mid] = max[mid] = c[mid];
        for (int i = mid - 1; i >= l; --i) {
            min[i] = std::min(min[i + 1], c[i]);
            max[i] = std::max(max[i + 1], c[i]);
        }
        min[mid + 1] = max[mid + 1] = c[mid + 1];
        rmax[c[mid + 1] - mid - 1 + n].emplace_back(c[mid + 1], mid + 1);
        for (int i = mid + 2; i <= r; ++i) {
            min[i] = std::min(min[i - 1], c[i]);
            max[i] = std::max(max[i - 1], c[i]);
            rmax[max[i] - i + n].emplace_back(min[i], i);
        }
        for (int i = mid; i >= l; --i) {
            if (min[i] != min[i + 1]) {
                int j = i + max[i] - min[i];
                if (j > mid && j <= r && min[j] > min[i] && max[j] < max[i]) {
                    R[i] = std::max(R[i], j);
                }
                j = min[i] - i + n;
                while (p[j] < std::ssize(rmax[j]) && rmax[j][p[j]].first > min[i]) {
                    ++p[j];
                }
                if (p[j]) {
                    int k = rmax[j][p[j] - 1].second;
                    if (max[k] > max[i]) R[i] = std::max(R[i], k);
                }
            }
        }
        for (int i = mid + 1; i <= r; ++i) {
            rmax[max[i] - i + n].clear();
            p[max[i] - i + n] = 0;
        }
    };
    work(work, 1, n);

    std::stack<int> st;
    int ans = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) {
        while (!st.empty() && st.top() <= i) st.pop();
        if (R[i]) {
            st.push(R[i]);
            ans = std::max(ans, int(st.size()));
        }
    }
    std::cout << (ans + 1) << '\n';
}

int main() {
    std::cin.tie(nullptr)->sync_with_stdio(false);
    int T = 1;
    std::cin >> T;
    while (T--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Fun Fact

The problem background is a tribute to the visual novel Symphonic Rain. I highly recommend you to play it!

Rate the problem!
Quality
  • 9 Excellent problem
  • 3 Good problem
  • 2 Average problem
  • 1 Bad problem
  • 4 Horrible problem
Difficulty
  • 1 Trivial problem
  • 1 Easy problem
  • 2 Average problem
  • 8 Hard problem
  • 4 Impossible problem

2187F2 - Al Fine (Counting Version)

  • Idea: StarSilk

  • Solution: StarSilk

  • Editorial: StarSilk

Solution

Construct the optimal tree by following the approach used in F1. Two child nodes aa and bb of a node are defined as entangled if and only if aa and bb appear in different orders in the two permutations. The maximal entangled set of a node's children is defined as the largest connected component formed by the entangled relationships among its children.

Theorem 11: Each maximal entangled set and the points in its subtree must form an interval in both DFS sequences.

Proof: Suppose one DFS sequence contains nodes a,b,ca,b,c such that aa and cc belong to the same maximal entanglement set while bb does not. Then in the other DFS sequence, all nodes after bb must appear after aa and before cc. Thus aa and cc cannot be entangled, leading to a contradiction.

Theorem 22: Among the maximal entangled sets of a leaf node, if any maximal entangled set contains only one node, then this maximal entangled set must be the last maximal entangled set to appear in both DFS sequences.

Proof: Suppose there exists a maximal entanglement set with a single node that is not the latest maximal entanglement set in either DFS sequence. Then all subsequent maximal entanglement sets must be able to be connected under this node, contradicting the optimal tree property.

Theorem 33: A maximal entanglement set must be connected under the same node in any valid tree.

Proof: For a pair of entangled child nodes x,yx,y of node aa in an optimal tree, let pxpx and pypy be the ancestors of xx and yy, respectively. Since pxpx and pypy cannot be points in the subtree of aa other than aa itself (otherwise it contradicts the optimal tree structure). Furthermore, since xx appears earlier than yy in some DFS sequence, traversing from xx to yy requires popping all other nodes in aa's subtree (because pypy is not within the maximal entangled group's range). This implies that traversing from xx to yy can only be achieved by popping pypy multiple times from xx and then attaching yy to pypy. Thus, pypy must be an ancestor of pxpx. Similarly, pxpx must also be an ancestor of pypy, so px=pypx=py.

Theorem 44: If a maximal entanglement set has size greater than 1, then on any valid tree, if the parent node of this maximal entanglement set is pp, then the subtree of all nodes within this maximal entanglement set must lie within the subtree of pp.

Proof: Suppose a node dd in the subtree of some node xx within the maximal entanglement set does not lie within the subtree of pp. Find a node yy entangled with xx. Then, in the DFS sequence where xx appears before yy, dd must lie between xx and yy, contradicting that dd is not in the subtree of pp.

Therefore, each maximal entanglement set greater than 11 can be collapsed into a single node, treated as a leaf attached to its parent. At this point, the two DFS sequences become identical. The number of solutions is thus equal to the number of trees where the DFS sequences perfectly match and every node that was originally in a maximal entanglement set remains a leaf in the new tree. For solutions within each maximal entanglement set, we can recursively compute the number of trees matching the DFS sequence for each node within that set. Multiplying these values yields the total number of valid trees.

Consider counting the number of trees where the DFS sequence is identical to the given sequence, and every node that was originally in a maximal entanglement set remains a leaf in the new tree. Treat the DFS process as a bracket sequence: entering a new node is treated as a left bracket, and exiting a node is treated as a right bracket. If a node is a leaf, then the next bracket after its left bracket must be a right bracket. Thus, the problem asks for the number of bracket sequences that satisfy having nn left brackets and right brackets, where the next bracket after the a1,a2,…,ama1,a2,…,amth left bracket is always a right bracket. A simple O(n2)O(n2) dynamic programming approach suffices to find the answer.

The overall time complexity is O(n2)O(n2).

Code (C++)
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
const long long mod=1000000007;
long long ans;
int a[5000],b[5000],pa[5000],pb[5000],tvis[5000],cvis[5000],zvis[5000];
vector<int>line[5001];
int dep[5001],cntsg[5001],wp[5001];
vector<int>vec;
long long dp[5001],tdp[5001];
long long solve(){
	long long i,j,n=vec.size(),csum;
	for(i=0;i<=n;i++)dp[i]=0;
	dp[0]=1;
	for(i=0;i<n;i++)
	{
		for(j=0;j<n;j++)tdp[j+vec[i]]=(tdp[j+vec[i]]+dp[j])%mod;
		csum=0;
		for(j=n;j>=0;j--)
		{
			csum=(csum+tdp[j])%mod;
			dp[j]=csum;
			tdp[j]=0;
		}
	}
	vec.clear();
	return dp[0];
}
void dfs(int t){
	vector<int>::iterator it;
	int i;
	for(i=0;i<cntsg[t];i++)vec.push_back(0);
	if(wp[t]==-1)ans=ans*solve()%mod;
	else
	{
		vec.push_back(1);
		dfs(wp[t]);
	}
	for(it=line[t].begin();it!=line[t].end();it++)
	{
		if(*it==wp[t])continue;
		vec.push_back(1);
		dfs(*it);
	}
}
int main(){
	ios::sync_with_stdio(false),cin.tie(0);
	int T,n,i,j,t,ccnt,r;
	for(cin>>T;T>0;T--)
	{
		cin>>n;
		for(i=0;i<n;i++)
		{
			cin>>a[i];
			a[i]--;
			pa[a[i]]=i;
		}
		for(i=0;i<n;i++)
		{
			cin>>b[i];
			b[i]--;
			pb[b[i]]=i;
		}
		for(i=0;i<n;i++)tvis[i]=0;
		for(i=n-1;i>-1;i--)
		{
			t=pb[a[i]];
			cntsg[i]=0;
			wp[i]=-1;
			for(j=0;j<n;j++)
			{
				cvis[j]=0;
				zvis[j]=0;
			}
			ccnt=0;
			r=-1;
			for(j=0;i+j<n&&t+j<n;j++)
			{
				if(cvis[a[i+j]]==0)ccnt++;
				cvis[a[i+j]]++;
				if(cvis[a[i+j]]==0)ccnt--;
				if(cvis[b[t+j]]==0)ccnt++;
				cvis[b[t+j]]--;
				if(cvis[b[t+j]]==0)ccnt--;
				if(ccnt==0)
				{
					zvis[i+j]=1;
					r=i+j;
				}
			}
			for(j=i+1;j<=r;j++)
			{
				if(tvis[j])
				{
					line[i].push_back(j);
					tvis[j]=0;
					if(zvis[j-1])
					{
						cntsg[i]++;
						wp[i]=j;
					}
					else wp[i]=-1;
				}
			}
			if(wp[i]!=-1)cntsg[i]--;
			tvis[i]=1;
		}
		cntsg[n]=0;
		wp[n]=-1;
		for(i=0;i<n;i++)
		{
			cvis[i]=0;
			zvis[i]=0;
		}
		ccnt=0;
		r=-1;
		for(i=0;i<n;i++)
		{
			if(cvis[a[i]]==0)ccnt++;
			cvis[a[i]]++;
			if(cvis[a[i]]==0)ccnt--;
			if(cvis[b[i]]==0)ccnt++;
			cvis[b[i]]--;
			if(cvis[b[i]]==0)ccnt--;
			if(ccnt==0)
			{
				zvis[i]=1;
				r=i;
			}
		}
		for(i=0;i<n;i++)
		{
			if(tvis[i])
			{
				line[n].push_back(i);
				tvis[i]=0;
				if(i==0||zvis[i-1])
				{
					cntsg[n]++;
					wp[n]=i;
				}
				else wp[n]=-1;
			}
		}
		if(wp[n]!=-1)cntsg[n]--;
		ans=1;
		dep[n]=0;
		dfs(n);
		for(i=0;i<=n;i++)line[i].clear();
		cout<<ans<<'\n';
	}
	return 0;
}
Bonus

Solve the problem in O(nlog2n)O(nlog2⁡n).

Rate the problem!
Quality
  • 2 Excellent problem
  • 2 Good problem
  • 0 Average problem
  • 1 Bad problem
  • 2 Horrible problem
Difficulty
  • 1 Trivial problem
  • 0 Easy problem
  • 0 Average problem
  • 2 Hard problem
  • 8 Impossible problem

2187G - Many Cartesian Trees

  • Idea: StarSilk

  • Solution: StarSilk

  • Editorial: StarSilk

Solution

Observation 0: If q1,q2,…,qnq1,q2,…,qn is valid, then q1modn+1,q2modn+1,…,qnmodn+1q1modn+1,q2modn+1,…,qnmodn+1 is another valid qq. This is obvious.

Therefore, we do not need the exact original labeling of values. We may assume qv=nqv=n.

Define Childv=u∣s[u][v]=1Childv=u∣s[u][v]=1; Define smnv=minu∈Childvusmnv=minu∈Childvu; Define smxv=maxu∈Childvusmxv=maxu∈Childvu.

Observation 1: If the value at index vv is nn, then the position of n−1n−1 must be either mnvmnv or mxvmxv.

Proof: If qx=n−1qx=n−1, and smnv<x<smxvsmnv<x<smxv. Then the parent node of at least one of mnvmnv and mxvmxv must be xx. This is contrary to the previous assumption.

So we only need to choose between these two candidates. We may assume qu=n−1qu=n−1.

Define an edge (u,parentu)(u,parentu) to cross index vv if uu and its parent lie on different sides of vv, in other words, (u−v)⋅(parentu−v)<0(u−v)⋅(parentu−v)<0.

Observation 2: In every Cartesian tree during the process, the edge between index uu (qu=n−1qu=n−1) and its parent (u,parentu)(u,parentu) never cross the index vv (qv=nqv=n).

Proof: For every node on a Cartesian tree, its parent node must be the closest node greater than it. If this edge (u,parentu)(u,parentu) crosses node v, then one side's closest point to node u becomes v, leading to a contradiction.

Now let's decide whether n−1n−1 is on the left or right. We are at index vv (treat it as value nn).

Case A: all extreme children are on one side. If smxv<vsmxv<v, then every child that ever attaches to vv lies left of vv. Therefore n−1n−1 must be on the left, and by Observation 1 it must be smnvsmnv. If smnv>vsmnv>v, similarly n−1n−1 must be on the right, hence it must be smxvsmxv.

Case B: children exist on both sides (smnv<v<smxvsmnv<v<smxv)

Observation 3: In the case B (smnv<v<smxvsmnv<v<smxv), among these two extreme children, one must have at least one parent edge crosses vv, and the other one mustn't.

Proof: One child qx=n−1qx=n−1 mustn't have a parent edge that crosses the index vv (qv=nqv=n) due to the observation 2. For the other child node qyqy, when qx=n−1qx=n−1 is the root node, the parent node of qyqy must be qxqx. They are on both sides of index vv (qv=nqv=n). Therefore, it has at least one parent edge that crosses the index vv (qv=nqv=n).

Similarly, define Fav=u∣s[v][u]=1Fav=u∣s[v][u]=1; Define fmnv=minu∈Favufmnv=minu∈Favu; Define fmxv=maxu∈Favufmxv=maxu∈Favu.

If fmxsmnv<vfmxsmnv<v, then smnvsmnv doesn't have a parent edge that crosses vv, then qsmnv=n−1qsmnv=n−1. And vice versa.

The time complexity is O(n2)O(n2). The bottleneck is preprocessing the input data.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
using i64 = long long;


signed main() {
    cin.tie(nullptr)->sync_with_stdio(false);

    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        vector<int> fa_mx(n + 1), fa_mn(n + 1, n);
        vector<int> son_mx(n + 1), son_mn(n + 1, n);
        vector<vector<int>> fa(n + 1), son(n + 1);
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            string s;
            cin >> s;
            s = ' ' + s;
            for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
                if (i == j or s[j] == '0') continue;
                int u = j, v = i;
                fa_mx[v] = max(fa_mx[v], u);
                fa_mn[v] = min(fa_mn[v], u);
                son_mx[u] = max(son_mx[u], v);
                son_mn[u] = min(son_mn[u], v);
            }
        }
        vector<int> a(n + 1);
        for (int p = n, val = n; ; val--) {
            a[p] = val;
            if (val == 1) break;
            int mn = son_mn[p], mx = son_mx[p];
            if (mx < p) {
                p = mn;
            } else if (mn > p) {
                p = mx;
            } else {
                int mx1 = fa_mx[mn];
                if (mx1 <= p) {
                    p = mn;
                } else {
                    int mn2 = fa_mn[mx];
                    assert(mn2 >= p);
                    p = mx;
                }
            }
        }
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            cout << a[i] << " \n"[i == n];
        }
    }
}

reirugan
2 months ago, show (+4) #
»
reirugan
2 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 7 →  
+28

Here's a braindead solution to Div1B where the correctness is completely obvious. It actually looks quite similar to the editorial solution and might end up producing the same output, but with a small sacrifice in runtime it becomes easier to prove.

Iterate over the 312312 possible combinations of the most significant bit you change in xx and yy (the extra 1 is from the possibility of not changing the value). If this changes a 00 to a 11 then the value increases, so we want to set all future bits to 00 to increase it by as little as possible. Otherwise, if this changes a 11 to a 00 then the value decreases, so we want to set all future bits to 11. However, if we are decreasing both values, this is invalid, so we will set the one with the earlier msb to all 11s and the one with the later msb to all 00s, thereby losing as little value as possible. Then, check if the resulting pp and qq satisfy the given condition and find the minimum over all satisfactory choices of msbs.

Submission: 360566950

Unfortunately I spent an hour doing some horrific casework before thinking of this, then I got nowhere on C, so I guess I'm back to CM for now, but I enjoyed the round nevertheless.

Edit: ok now that I think about it, the proof is not as trivial as I thought it was, since there are some tricky cases where the bits in between the two msbs are 1. There's an easy way to take care of this, though. If you fix the most significant bits you change, now you can greedily assign everything lower.

→ Reply

    »
    unrealassembler
    2 months ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    unrealassembler
    2 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Can you elaborate a bit on your edit? I'm not sure what exactly changed since you're fixing the MSB and assigning lower bits in both the cases.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      reirugan
      2 months ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      reirugan
      2 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Something like, suppose i = position of msb you change in x, j = position of msb you change in y. WLOG, let i > j, that is, i is more significant than j. Maybe x = 6 and y = 3, for example. Furthermore, suppose that x_i = 1 and y_i = 0.

      Then assigning

      p = ...0111111

      q = ...xxx1000

      where x can be anything, won't necessarily be optimal because in particular, it's not always legal since the x's might be 1s. But you can just assign greedily where on each bit you ensure it's legal, and since you already fixed the msbs, it's easy to see how you want to greedily assign each value.

      Anyways, even the original solution still ACs, it's just nontrivial to prove now; you have to show that all choices of i, j that cause this issue are suboptimal, which is probably true by some exchange argument. The greedy is fairly easy to prove, I think.

    My Code I did my solution based on this observation which is O(1). Basically if q=y then you can ALWAYS make the lowerbits (starting from the highest same bit) of x to the complement of lower bits of y, by subtracting.

Golovanov399

+25

In div1D, I liked to think of it as, if y=0y=0 and x=1x=1, "our current sum is 0+1+…+k0+1+…+k, and by default we are adding the next number/removing the last number". If we read 0 from the string, we do what we are going to do by default, 1 changes this default option and applies the new default immediately. After that it's still the same knapsack etc, but it required me to handle the case where we want to remove the last number from "our current sum is 00" separately, so it's not the cleanest way to describe the solution, but it's what I had in mind when solving this.

In div1F1, I copy-pasted the PQ tree from yosupo judge, and then wrote some DP on it, but unlike normal tree DPs, this one required handling the siblings as well.

All in all, thanks for the contest! D was super cool

→ Reply

0

My (maybe overcomplicated) DP solution to div1 B / div2 D.

Let xixi and yiyi be the ii-th lowest bit of xx and yy (0≤xi,yi≤1)(0≤xi,yi≤1).

Let the DP state be D[i,j,k](0≤i≤31,−1≤j,k≤1)D[i,j,k](0≤i≤31,−1≤j,k≤1). ii is the bit index (counting from the lowest). j=1j=1 means the value of pp has a carry in the digit ii; j=−1j=−1 means pp has a borrow in the digit ii; j=0j=0 means the otherwise. The same applies to kk, for the value of qq. D[i,j,k]D[i,j,k] is the minimum deviation from xx and yy (i.e. the value of |x−p|+|y−q||x−p|+|y−q|) that's required to arrive at the state.

Initially, D[0,0,0]=0D[0,0,0]=0 and D[i,j,k]=∞D[i,j,k]=∞ for the rest.

Let's think about the state transition from D[i,j,k](i≤30)D[i,j,k](i≤30). The current value of pp and qq at the bit ii, including a carry or a borrow, is: a=xi+ja=xi+j and b=yi+kb=yi+k (−1≤a,b≤2)(−1≤a,b≤2).

Let's bruteforce and consider all the combinations of changes to pp and qq at the bit ii: you might want to add 11 to or subtract 11 from the ii-th bit of pp and qq. For all −1≤c≤1−1≤c≤1 and −1≤d≤1−1≤d≤1, the new ii-th bit of pp and qq are: a′=a+c,b′=b+d(−2≤a′,b′≤3)a′=a+c,b′=b+d(−2≤a′,b′≤3). If a′&1=1a′&1=1 and b′&1=1b′&1=1, then these don't satisfy the problem condition, so skip. Otherwise, this change pattern can be viable. Note that the carry or the borrow of a′a′ and b′b′ affects the next bit only. The effect of the next bit can be denoted as: j′=⌊a′/2⌋,k′=⌊b′/2⌋j′=⌊a′/2⌋,k′=⌊b′/2⌋. The cost of this change (the deviation from xx and yy) can be calculated as: s=(|c|+|d|)⋅2is=(|c|+|d|)⋅2i. With that, update the DP state as follows:

D[i+1,j′,k′]←min(D[i+1,j′,k′],D[i,j,k]+s).D[i+1,j′,k′]←min(D[i+1,j′,k′],D[i,j,k]+s).

After all the transitions, the minimum deviation from xx and yy can be obtained as D[31,0,0]D[31,0,0]. You can recover the value of pp and qq by backtracking the DP table, if you leave enough information as you make the transition.

→ Reply

For div2D, abs(x-p) could be x-p, p-x, 0 depending on x > p, x < p, x = p maintain a DP with three states, bit position, state for p {whether, > < or =}, state for q there are three possibilities for bit assignment for p&q=0 at position j get the new state as bit is assigned and maintain current minimum as current state establishes the inequality to calculate abs(x-p) + abs(x-q), store the minimum reached for every such state and trace back to get values of p and q

    ok so as a pupil myself what i understood first simply by observation and casework that if i choose say my p as x then i can find a q which is the optimal solution this was done purely based on casework. Now once i fix say p=x now i want a q which is as close as possible to y so to minimize the sum so to do that i can only use those bits which are unset in x since we want p&q==0 so i crate a mask where those bits are set which are unset in x now we build our q first as close to y as possible to do this we would build two numbers l and h where l is the largest number closest to y and h be the smallest number greater than y how you build you can see my code it would be much more clear to you

      0

      but how can you assume only if you fix p = x (or vice versa) then it's optimal, because this is observation, but you can't prove what if p is also as close as possible to x and y is also as close as possible to y but none are exactly equal. Like we need a way to prove that it's always optimal if either p = x or q = y and any deviation will only increase the answer.

        alphoenix
        2 months ago, show (+1) #

        alphoenix
        2 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        I solved it with DP as abs(x-p) could be x-p, p-x, 0 depending on x > p, x < p, x = p maintain a DP with three states, bit position, state for p {whether, > < or =}, state for q there are three possibilities for bit assignment for p&q=0 at position j get the new state as bit is assigned and maintain current minimum as current state establishes the inequality to calculate abs(x-p) + abs(x-q), store the minimum reached for every such state and trace back to get values of p and q. Why this works is because if you are already larger at a position j, you will always be larger later and if you are already smaller at a position j, you will be always smaller later. That solves it by saving to check all the permutations in 32*3*3*4.

Can anyone explain me why this code gives wrong answer on some test cases.

#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <utility>
#include <cmath>
#include <queue>
#include <map>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int tt;
    cin >> tt;
    while (tt--) {
        int n;
        cin >> n;
        vector<int> a(n);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            cin >> a[i];
        }
        vector<int> cpy = a;
        sort(cpy.begin(), cpy.end());
        if (cpy == a) {
            cout << -1 << '\n';
            continue;
        }
        map<int, queue<int>> mp1, mp2;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            mp1[a[i]].push(i);
            mp2[cpy[i]].push(i);
        }
        int ans = 1e9;
        int mx = cpy[n - 1], mn = cpy[0];
        for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
            int f = mp1[a[i]].front();
            int s = mp2[a[i]].front();
            if (f == s) {
                continue;
            } else {
                int idx = mp2[a[i]].front();
                int _max = max(abs(mn - a[idx]), abs(mx - a[idx]));
                ans = min(ans, _max);
            }
            mp1[a[i]].pop();
            mp2[a[i]].pop();
        }
        cout << ans << '\n';
    }
}
→ Reply

ibrm

ibrm
2 months ago, hide # |
 
+36

Any intuition behind div1.D? After reading the editorial it just blows my mind how one can come up with this in contest.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    FetFot
    2 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    FetFot
    2 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +23

    If xx must be aa or bb, then (x−a)⋅(x−b)=0(x−a)⋅(x−b)=0 must hold.

    There is no need to rewrite the formula in this magical way. Instead of handling two separate "if" cases, we can pack both possibilities into a single, initially messy equation:

    (ci−ci−1−x)⋅(ci+ci−1−y)=0(ci−ci−1−x)⋅(ci+ci−1−y)=0

    Expanding this gives us a much more useful form:

    (ci−ci−1)(ci+ci−1)−x(ci+ci−1)−y(ci−ci−1)+xy=0(ci−ci−1)(ci+ci−1)−x(ci+ci−1)−y(ci−ci−1)+xy=0
    c2i−c2i−1−ci(x+y)−ci−1(x−y)+xy=0ci2−ci−12−ci(x+y)−ci−1(x−y)+xy=0

    Now, the magic happens when you sum this up for all ii. The c2c2 telescoping terms in the middle cancel out, allowing you to isolate ∑ci∑ci.

    I hope this helps!

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      ibrm
      2 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      ibrm
      2 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Yeah, thank you very much. I guess intuition was to try to join the 2 equations into one, and see what we can get from there. Very interesting problem.

__baozii__ What is the name of the trick? They way I have solved this question is through dealing vertex all togather based on depths for each vertex(iterating over every subtree except the one with max depth). You may see the code 360582035

This is O(n)O(n) if I'm not mistaken.

»
Ashwanth.K
2 months ago, show #
»
Ashwanth.K
2 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+14

Div 1 C Jerry and Tom Bonus Version:

  • We can precompute distances of each node from root.
  • For every node, we need to compute how many pairs (u,v)(u,v) in its subtree, with same depth (depth[u]=depth[v])(depth[u]=depth[v]). which is doable using small to large technique in O(nlog2(n))O(nlog2(n)). rest all parts of problem can be done in O(N)O(N)
  • To optimize this, lets consider a auxiliary tree for all nodes of depth = d. Its a known fact that if # nodes = k, worst case number of nodes for auxiliary tree is 2k−12k−1.

  • So if auxiliary tree is constructed, then we shall process the tree, and for each node uu, maintain:

    • Number of nodes of current depth d, in its subtree (good nodes)
    • Number of pairs of nodes (good nodes) in its subtree. (this is important)
  • If observed, the nodes of auxiliary tree are just a compressed versions of nodes of actual tree. To propagate the answers from auxiliary tree to main tree, we shall use difference arrays and prefix sum ideas to add values in range [Aux[U]...Aux[par[U]])[Aux[U]...Aux[par[U]])

Construct Auxiliary Tree:

  • Remaining part is to construct Auxiliary tree from depth d to depth d+1, which is also doable in total of O(N).
    • Iterate on nodes of depth d.
    • If they are leaf nodes, remove them and keep removing parents (if orphaned — no children)
    • Iterate on nodes of depth d again, (now to compress paths). If parent has only 1 child, remove parent, attach to grandparent (recursive).
    • Now add nodes of d+1 depth
    • Iterate on nodes of depth d+1, (now to compress paths). If parent has only 1 child, remove parent, attach to grandparent (recursive).

Sorry for overkilling it, but good exercise.

→ Reply

    dooglius
    2 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Upsolving I reduced to subset sum which has an O(nn√w)O(nnw) solution. Interestingly, it looks like this is tied for the fastest solution, which I wasn't trying to go for. The idea is to represent cici in terms of sums of zizi where zizi is the xorsum of s1⊕⋯⊕sis1⊕⋯⊕si, then ff can be written in terms of just ∑izi∑izi. Each chain of 00/11 in ss after a ′?′′?′ can be represented as two possibilities based on the value of the ′?′′?′ which reduces to subset-sum.

    → Reply

If anyone wants a dp solution for div 2 D :Problem Link

Here is my code:{If explanation needed , ping me} .

int dp[35][4][4]; 

pair<int,int>  best[35][4][4]; 
vector<pair<int,int>> choice={{0,1},{1,0},{0,0}}; 
int vis[35][4][4];
int timer=0; 
 int x,y;
/*0->equal , 1->less,2->greater*/
int rec(int level,int ps,int qs){
	if(level<0) return 0; 
	if(vis[level][ps][qs]==timer) return dp[level][ps][qs];
	
	int ans=1e16;
		int x_bit=((x&(1LL<<level))>0?1:0); 
		int y_bit=((y&(1LL<<level))>0?1:0);
		int val=(1LL<<level);  
	for(auto &[p_bit,q_bit]:choice){
		int new_ps,new_qs; 
		int cost=0; 
		int p_cost=(x_bit-p_bit)*val;
		int q_cost=(y_bit-q_bit)*val; 
		
		if(ps==0) cost+=abs(p_cost);
		else if(ps==1) cost+=p_cost; 
		else cost-=p_cost; 
		
		if(qs==0) cost+=abs(q_cost);
		else if(qs==1) cost+=q_cost; 
		else cost-=q_cost;
		
		new_ps=(!ps?((p_cost>0)?1:2):ps);
		if(x_bit==p_bit && !ps) new_ps=0;  
		new_qs=(!qs?((q_cost>0)?1:2):qs); 
		if(y_bit==q_bit && !qs) new_qs=0; 
		int temp=rec(level-1,new_ps,new_qs)+cost; 
		if(ans>temp){
			ans=temp; 
			best[level][ps][qs]=make_pair(p_bit,q_bit); 
		} 
		 
	}
	vis[level][ps][qs]=timer; 
	return dp[level][ps][qs]=ans; 
	
}
int p_ans,q_ans; 
void genrate(int level,int ps,int qs,int &p,int &q){
	if(level<0) return; 
	auto &[p_bit,q_bit]=best[level][ps][qs]; 
	p|=(1LL<<level)*p_bit; 
	q|=(1LL<<level)*q_bit; 
	
	int n_ps=ps,n_qs=qs; 
	
	int x_bit=((x&(1LL<<level))>0?1:0); 
	int y_bit=((y&(1LL<<level))>0?1:0);
	
	if(ps==0){
		n_ps=(p_bit>x_bit?2:(x_bit!=p_bit?1:0)); 
		
	}
	if(!qs){
		n_qs=(q_bit>y_bit?2:(y_bit!=q_bit?1:0)); 
	}
	genrate(level-1,n_ps,n_qs,p,q); 
}
void solve(){
 cin>>x>>y; 
 	timer++;  
 	 p_ans=0,q_ans=0; 
 	int ans=rec(32,0,0);
	genrate(32,0,0,p_ans,q_ans); 
	
 	cout<<p_ans<<" "<<q_ans<<'\n'; 
 
    
}
 
signed main(){
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(0);
    cout.tie(0);
    int t=1; 
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
     solve();
    }
    
    return 0;
}
→ Reply

-8

For 2187C/2188E, won't it sometimes be optimal to pick a node that's not the farthest?

Consider the following graph:

3->8 6->9

(Note that 1->2, 2->3, 3->4, ..., 9-10 also exist).

Say Jerry is at 3 while Tom is at 6 and it's Tom's turn. Tom would actually benefit to go from 6 to 7 rather than 6 to 9, since it prevents Jerry from going to 8 (Tom would go there next move) and forces him to, for the rest of the game, move one step at a time. If Tom moved to 9, Jerry could have moved one step at a time and the game would have lasted longer. If Tom stayed, then Jerry could go to 8 and go on to actually win the game.

Let's continue the same graph and look at it from Jerry's perspective. This time, he is at 3 and Tom is at 9. If Jerry moves to 8, Tom could stay and then Jerry would lose next turn. But he can also move to 4, and Tom would stay stationary for 5 more moves for Jerry to catch up. Thus, it would be best for him to go slow.

This example serves as a counter-example right?

→ Reply

    case3
    2 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    case3
    2 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    The weightage of subtraction here is dependent too much on the highest first conflict. 2^n will always be more than 2^0 + 2^1 + ... 2^(n-1). So assigning this bit to either one of the number will change the answer from there or take one of the numbers a little bigger.

    I solved it by guessing from all 4 options that can happen on the first MSB match.

0

A slightly different intuition for solving the d1D/d2F problem. If we consider the fixed string rr, we can see that c[i]c[i] contributes to the total sum yy with a coefficient equal to 00 or 11, with a coefficient of 00 if the number of ones in the prefix ss is even and 11 if it is odd (i.e., xorxor of prefix ii). If, after this observation, we consider by analogy what happens to the xx's on prefixes with different xorxor, we can see that as long as the number of ones on prefixes ii and i+1i+1 is the same, c[i+1]c[i+1] contributes one xx more to the sum than c[i]c[i]. after we encounter another unit, this type of operation will reverse all our xx's and we will lose the last member of the arithmetic progression. For example, let it be equal to k∗xk∗x, then as long as the prefixes' xorxor values are equal, we will continue to lose (k−1)∗x(k−1)∗x, (k−2)∗x(k−2)∗x, and so on for the subsequent terms of the arithmetic progression, until we encounter a unit again, at which point we will continue the arithmetic progression, but only from the last term that was not subtracted. This means that the total sum of all c[i]c[i] is yy multiplied by the number of prefixes with xor=1xor=1 plus an arithmetic progression with a step of xx and a length equal to the difference between the number of prefixes with xor=0xor=0 and xor=1xor=1.

I apologize, this was translated using a translation tool.

→ Reply

Could anyone please explain where I am going wrong with this solution for Div1 D.

My code keeps failing

Between two question marks, the value of c at the position is a linear function of the value at the question mark. Therefore when summing over all the sequences we can substitute the average value of c at the question mark in the linear function.

But the average value at the question mark is (x+y)/2 because it can be either 0 or 1 for every possible preceding sequence. So we calculate the sequence as normal, replacing c with (x+y)/2 at every question mark, summing the obtained values of c, then multiplying by 2^(no. of question marks)

For (x+y)/2 you can use the modular inverse of 2

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Furryfox
    2 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Furryfox
    2 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You are treating this question as if it asks to compute ∑completionsf(s)∑completionsf(s) when ∑k∈values that f(s) can takek∑k∈values that f(s) can takek is being asked. They differ when two completions yield the same value of ff.

    Counter-example for your approach: nn = 2, xx = 1, yy = 2, ss = ??

    List Completions are as follows:

    00: c1c1 = 1, c2c2 = 2, ⇒⇒ ff = 1+2 = 3.

    01: c1c1 = 1, c2c2 = 1, ⇒⇒ ff = 2.

    10: c1c1 = 2, c2c2 = 3, ⇒⇒ ff = 5.

    11: c1c1 = 2, c2c2 = 0, ⇒⇒ ff = 2.

    Cool Integer list is then {2,3,5} and the required answer becomes 10. However, multiplying by 2count of ?2count of ?, you implicitly count each completion, which forces you to count ff = 2 twice, creating a mismatch.

    → Reply

For 2A, an alternate solution can be like this: 1. The first element is always ceil(n/2). 2. For every next element, you can add/subtract index i of the prev_element to the prev element alternatively to get the whole permutation.

import math
test_cases = int(input())

for i in range(0, test_cases):
    n = int(input())
    prev_element = math.ceil(n/2)
    print(prev_element, end = ' ')
    for i in range(1, n):
        if i%2 == 1:
            prev_element += i
        else:
            prev_element -=i
        print(prev_element, end = ' ')
    print('\n')
→ Reply
»

can somebody Prove to me why this:

auto dfs = [&](auto &&self, int u, int p) -> map<int, int> {
        pa[u] = p;
        map<int, int> mp;
        mp[dep[u]] = 1;
        for (auto v : g[u]) if (v != p) {
            dep[v] = dep[u] + 1;
            auto nmp = self(self, v, u);
            if (mp.size() < nmp.size()) swap(mp, nmp);
            for (auto &[d, f] : nmp) {
                ans += 1LL * f * mp[d] * (d &mdash; dep[u]);
                mp[d] += f;
            }
            sz[u] += sz[v];
        }
        return mp;
};

Its complexity is O(n⋅log2n)n⋅log2n)).

Another solution for A-Divisible Permutation (C++)

One array only!.

Construct the answer in reverse order.

Take: q = [n, 1, n-1, 2, n-2, 3, ...] Then the adjacent differences in q are: n-1, n-2, n-3, ..., 1

After reversing q, the difference at position i becomes exactly i, so |p[i] — p[i+1]| = i, which is divisible by i.

#include <bits/stdc++.h>

using namespace std;

void solve(){
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    int pos = n / 2;
    if(n%2 == 1)pos ++;
    vector<int> np;
    for (size_t i = 0; i <= pos; i++)
    {
        np.push_back(n - i);
        np.push_back(i + 1);
    }
    for(auto i = n - 1;i >=0 ;i--)cout << np[i] << " ";
    cout << endl;
}
int main(){
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--)solve();
    return 0;
}
